title: Hormuz Traffic Surges but Confidence Lags, Putin Admits Fuel Crisis, and Oil Slides Below $75
subhead: Tanker transits through Hormuz hit their highest weekly count since February, but war-risk premiums and disputed shipping lanes keep full recovery out of reach. Meanwhile, Putin publicly acknowledges Russian fuel shortages for the first time, oil prices sit near three-month lows, and Doha talks on the Iran deal's most contentious issues are set to begin.

sections.heading: Hormuz Rebound Is Real — But Incomplete and Fragile
sections.body:
Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz recorded 125 weekly transits in the week of June 15-21, the highest since the Iran crisis began in late February, according to CryptoBriefing citing ship-tracking data. Iraqi Oil Minister Basem Al-Abadi confirmed that Iraqi oil fields are prepared for a production ramp-up, and SOMO has begun coordinating tanker nominations for upcoming loadings. Iraq's exports had collapsed from roughly 93 million barrels per month pre-crisis to approximately 10 million barrels in April — a near-90% drop that gutted Baghdad's primary revenue source.

But the Cyprus Mail reports a far more cautious picture beneath the headline numbers. Arabian Gulf crude exports recovered to 6.97 million barrels per day by June 24, but that remained roughly 40% below the 2023-2025 average. Tanker transits were still 58% below pre-war levels by end of June. The IMO's phased evacuation plan for stranded seafarers was paused after an attack on a vessel in the Gulf of Oman. Iran insists safe passage is only guaranteed through its approved routes, creating a governance vacuum where shippers must choose between Iranian-controlled lanes and riskier alternatives.

The 60-day ceasefire negotiated under the US-Iran MOU provides a window, but not a resolution. As the Cyprus Mail analysis put it: 'A ceasefire can reopen the passage, but it cannot by itself restore confidence.' War-risk premiums for Gulf transits remain several times higher than pre-war levels, according to S&P Global, even after easing from their March peaks.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between transits and trust is the story. 125 weekly crossings is a recovery signal, but 58% below normal traffic means the market is pricing in a persistent risk of disruption. Until the governance dispute over who controls Hormuz shipping lanes is resolved — which is what Tuesday's Doha talks are partly about — the Strait will remain in a liminal state: open enough to lower prices, not open enough to restore normal flows.

sections.heading: Oil Slides Below $75 as Geopolitical Risk Premium Deflates
sections.body:
Crude oil prices continued their post-MOU slide. Brent crude traded near $74 per barrel and WTI near $71 on Tuesday, according to the Financial Express, with both benchmarks well below the psychologically important $75 mark. Oil prices had already fallen 5% to a three-month low following the initial US-Iran ceasefire announcement, per the headlines data, and the downward pressure has continued as markets price in the return of Iranian and Iraqi supply.

ING's head of commodities strategy Warren Patterson noted in a Seeking Alpha analysis that the sell-off may be overdone, pointing to Commitments of Traders data showing ICE Brent consumer longs remain well below pre-war levels. The sharp drop reflects markets pricing in a quick recovery of Persian Gulf supplies under the MOU — even though actual throughput remains well below normal.

Gold and silver have also been moving in parallel. Spot gold fell below $4,000 an ounce — an eight-month low — while silver dropped below $58, according to GoodReturns. Both precious metals have declined roughly 15% this quarter, partly reflecting the deflation of geopolitical risk premiums that had been baked into safe-haven demand. Markets are now pricing in three Federal Reserve rate hikes this year, with the first potentially in September, adding further pressure to commodities.
sections.whyItMatters: The oil price decline tells you the market believes the MOU will hold — or at least that global demand is soft enough to absorb even a disrupted Hormuz. But ING's caution about positioning suggests the sell-off has outrun the fundamentals. If the Doha talks stall or another tanker incident occurs, the risk premium could snap back quickly.

sections.heading: Putin Acknowledges Fuel Crisis as Drone Strikes Set Refinery Ablaze
sections.body:
Russian President Vladimir Putin publicly acknowledged for the first time that Russia faces a 'certain deficit' of fuel, speaking to a Russian state TV reporter on Sunday, according to the AP. The admission came as Ukrainian drones set fire to the Slavyansk-na-Kubani refinery in Krasnodar, one of southern Russia's major facilities, processing close to 4 million tons of crude per year. Debris from downed drones killed one person and wounded another. Zelenskyy also claimed a strike on a refinery in the Yaroslavl region, roughly 700 km from the Ukrainian border.

The drone campaign has doubled in intensity since the start of 2026, according to Reuters data cited by GDN Online. The number of drone strikes on Russian refineries has doubled, leading to full or partial shutdowns and a decline in production of gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel. Fuel shortages have been reported in around a dozen Russian regions, with rationing imposed in several areas. Wholesale prices for AI-95 petrol and diesel on the St Petersburg exchange rose 10% in the first half of June and remain far from stabilizing, per traders.

Russia's response has been to slash fuel quality standards. Kommersant reported that the government is allowing production of Euro-2 standard gasoline and diesel — containing up to 150 parts per million of sulphur, roughly 15 times the maximum permitted in Europe, China, and India — and allowing higher shares of aromatic hydrocarbons linked to health issues. The standard, banned since 2013, could be permitted through July 2027. Putin also told party members at the United Russia congress: 'Yes, we see the problems, we are aware of them and are responding to them,' according to The Independent.
sections.whyItMatters: Putin's public admission of fuel shortages is significant — it concedes that Ukraine's drone campaign is achieving its strategic objective of degrading Russia's domestic energy infrastructure. The decision to legalize Euro-2 fuel with 15x the sulphur content of European standards is a clear sign of desperation. It means Russian civilians and military vehicles alike are running on lower-quality fuel, and the environmental and health consequences will accumulate. Ukraine has turned Russian fuel into a political problem for the Kremlin, not just a logistical one.

sections.heading: Iran to Receive $6-12 Billion in Unfrozen Assets as Doha Talks Loom
sections.body:
Iran is set to receive between $6 billion and $12 billion of its frozen assets following the memorandum of understanding with the United States, according to CryptoBriefing citing Iranian politician Masoud Pezeshkian. The assets, originally frozen in 2018 and transferred to Qatar in 2023, are designated for humanitarian purposes under strict oversight. Iran's UN ambassador stated that only Iran will control how the unfrozen assets are used, denying that Washington would have any say over the funds or that they must be used to buy US commodities.

The asset release coincides with a pivotal moment in the diplomatic process. Technical teams from Iran and the US are expected to meet in Doha on Tuesday to work on implementing the interim peace deal, according to Reuters. The talks will focus on shipping security in the Strait of Hormuz and the broader framework for a nuclear agreement. A June 30 deadline for a final nuclear deal has been referenced in reporting, though it remains unclear whether this is a hard deadline or aspirational.

The big question is whether the asset release and technical talks translate into durable de-escalation. Iran has linked any final nuclear deal to Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon, according to Hezbollah statements cited by Reuters. The Revolutionary Guards, who control Iran's ballistic missile arsenal and answer only to Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, are wielding greater influence than before. RUSI's HA Hellyer noted that 'a drawn-out negotiation accompanied by controlled pressure in the Strait can work to Iran's advantage.'
sections.whyItMatters: The $6-12 billion asset release is the tangible financial dimension of the MOU — and it is also the most politically contentious. Iran insists on full sovereignty over the funds, while the US faces domestic criticism for any appearance of paying Tehran. The Doha technical talks will determine whether the ceasefire framework can survive contact with the hardest unresolved issues: who controls Hormuz, whether Iran's nuclear program will be permanently constrained, and whether Iran's linkage to Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon can be separated.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals Pricing Plan Hits G7 Resistance
sections.body:
The Trump administration's push to create a Western critical minerals trading bloc with regulated pricing is facing skepticism from G7 allies and division within the mining industry, according to Reuters and ChemAnalyst. The proposal, originally introduced by Vice President JD Vance in February, would use a DARPA-developed AI pricing model to set minimum price supports, subsidies, standardized market rules, and long-term government-backed purchase agreements for minerals like cobalt, lithium, nickel, graphite, and rare earth elements.

European officials have reportedly raised concerns about who would absorb the cost of paying higher prices for critical minerals and whether financial incentives should extend beyond mining companies to processors, manufacturers, and end-product producers. There are also questions about the transparency of the DARPA pricing model, the methodology for determining 'fair' mineral prices, and which authority would oversee enforcement.

Meanwhile, Canada announced a C$7 million grant for a Greenland molybdenum mine at the G7, according to Mother Jones reporting on critical minerals deals — part of a broader Western push to develop non-Chinese supply chains. But the bilateral approach underscores the gap between ambition and agreement: without a unified G7 pricing framework, individual nations are cutting their own deals.
sections.whyItMatters: The critical minerals pricing plan is the most ambitious Western attempt to challenge China's dominance in strategic mineral supply chains — but the skepticism from allies and industry suggests it may end up as another framework without enforcement. China's ability to produce below cost has hollowed out Western mining investment; the question is whether government-managed pricing can reverse that without creating a permanently subsidized industry. For now, the answer from G7 partners is: show us the math.

sections.heading: Iraq Pushes for Hormuz Reopening and Alternative Routes
sections.body:
Iraq is pressing for a full reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and simultaneously exploring alternative export routes to reduce its dependence on the chokepoint, according to CryptoBriefing. Iraqi oil exports collapsed from roughly 93 million barrels per month pre-crisis to approximately 10 million barrels in April 2026 — a nearly 90% drop that devastated the country's fiscal position, given its overwhelming dependence on petroleum revenues.

Baghdad has been negotiating with Iran on reopening and is also pursuing alternatives including Iraq's Umm Qasr port and the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey's Mediterranean coast. The Rumaila oil field experienced shutdowns in early March due to transit disruptions. Iraqi Oil Minister Al-Abadi has confirmed that Iraq's oil fields are prepared for a production ramp-up, but the increase is described as gradual and dependent on passage conditions through the Strait.

Iraq's search for alternatives is a structural shift that will outlast the current crisis. Even if Hormuz fully reopens, the experience of losing 90% of export capacity for three months has demonstrated a vulnerability that Baghdad cannot afford to ignore. The Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline and Umm Qasr port represent long-term infrastructure bets that could reshape Iraq's export geography — if they can be built and secured.
sections.whyItMatters: Iraq is the canary in the coal mine for Hormuz dependency. When the Strait closed, Iraq lost nearly all of its revenue almost overnight — a sharper hit than any other Gulf producer because it had no meaningful alternative export capacity. Iraq's diversification push, if it materializes, could be one of the war's most lasting structural legacies: a major Gulf producer permanently reducing its exposure to Hormuz.

bottomLine: The Strait of Hormuz is crawling back to life — 125 weekly transits, exports at 7 million barrels per day — but the recovery is incomplete and fragile, with traffic still 58% below pre-war norms and war-risk premiums several times higher than before the conflict. Oil near $74 Brent reflects market confidence in the MOU, but that confidence could reverse quickly if the Doha talks stall. Putin's unprecedented public admission of fuel shortages and Russia's rollback to Euro-2 fuel standards with 15x the sulphur content of European norms underscore that Ukraine's drone campaign is achieving strategic effects far beyond the battlefield. And as Iran prepares to receive $6-12 billion in unfrozen assets and technical teams head to Doha, the hardest questions — who controls Hormuz, what constraints apply to Iran's nuclear program, and whether Tehran's linkage to Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon can be managed — remain entirely unresolved.
sources: AP News, CryptoBriefing, Cyprus Mail, Financial Express, GoodReturns, Seeking Alpha (ING/Warren Patterson), Reuters, Kommersant, GDN Online, The Independent, CBC News, S&P Global, Reuters (via headlines), ChemAnalyst, Mother Jones
